coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence quenching capillary analysis for determining trace-level nitrite in food based on the citric acid/ethylenediamine nanodots/nitrite reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Bao-Lin
Li, Yong-Sheng
Gao, Xiu-Feng
coredata.dc:description: We found that nitrite after protonation can react with amine radical on citric acid/ethylenediamine carbon nanodots (CA/EDA-CDs) to form nitrosamines, and fluorescence quenching of CA/EDA-CDs occurred during this process. Using the reaction mechanism a fluorescence quenching capillary analysis (FQCA) was developed. After optimized reaction conditions, the following results were obtained: the required concentration of CA/EDA-CDs was 12 mg/L, HCl concentration was 32 mmol/L, and the reaction conducted in room temperature for 20 min. Under optimized conditions, FQCA has a linear response in 20–500 μg/L in which RSD was less 4.5% (n = 11), the detection limit was 6.5 μg/L and the recovery was in 95–105%. The measured results were consistent with the national standard method. FQCA has been used for determining nitrite in foods and nature waters. The capillary in FQCA was used as the container for CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − reaction and NO2 − determination, and realized trace-level analysis for micro-volume samples (<10 μL/time).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite
Citric acid
Ethylenediamine
Carbon nanodots
Fluorescence capillary analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence spectrum related to the reaction system
Effect of temperature for reaction system
Tests on real samples and comparison

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reaction process illustration between nitrite with diethylenediamine carbon nanodots.

Fluorescence spectra of EDA/CA-CDs and CA-CDs. (a) Excitation and emission spectra of CA/EDA-CDs; (b) Emission spectra obtained by mixing CA/EDA-CDs with different concentration of nitrites; (c) Spectral curves of EDA/CA-CDs under different exciting light; (d) Spectral curves of CA-CDs under different exciting light.

Optimization for the reaction conditions. (a) effect of pH on the fluorescence of CA/EDA-CDs; (b) influence of CA/EDA-CDs concentration; (c) effect of CA/EDA-CDs reagent on the volume ratio of the sample; (d) effect of reaction temperature; (e) change of the fluorescence value of CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − reaction liquid with time; (f) change of the fluorescence value of CA/EDA-CDs at room temperature with time.

Effects of system acidity and the granular and powdered active carbons on nitrite determination. (a) Effect of pH on the fluorescence of CA/EDA-CDs; (b) influence of powdered active carbon; (c) the obtained result with granular active-carbon to treat the nitrite samples.
Effects of coexisting ions on CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − (0.5 mg/L) reaction system.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Adjusting the sample pH to 7; RD (ΔF2 − ΔF1)/ΔF1 was the relative deviation which was created by coexisting ions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determined results and contrastive tests for actual samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The extracted sample was diluted 10 times in the fluorescence cell method.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence quenching capillary analysis for determining trace-level nitrite in food based on the citric acid/ethylenediamine nanodots/nitrite reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: West China School of Preclinical and Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: West China School of Preclinical and Forensic Medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We found that nitrite after protonation can react with amine radical on citric acid/ethylenediamine carbon nanodots (CA/EDA-CDs) to form nitrosamines, and fluorescence quenching of CA/EDA-CDs occurred during this process. Using the reaction mechanism a fluorescence quenching capillary analysis (FQCA) was developed. After optimized reaction conditions, the following results were obtained: the required concentration of CA/EDA-CDs was 12 mg/L, HCl concentration was 32 mmol/L, and the reaction conducted in room temperature for 20 min. Under optimized conditions, FQCA has a linear response in 20–500 μg/L in which RSD was less 4.5% (n = 11), the detection limit was 6.5 μg/L and the recovery was in 95–105%. The measured results were consistent with the national standard method. FQCA has been used for determining nitrite in foods and nature waters. The capillary in FQCA was used as the container for CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − reaction and NO2 − determination, and realized trace-level analysis for micro-volume samples (<10 μL/time).

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite exists in a variety of foods and natural waters and can transform into various nitrogen oxides after entering the human body (Lundberg, Weitzberg, & Gladwin, 2008). Presently, there is controversy on whether nitrite affects health (Erkekoglü & Baydar, 2010; Bryan, Alexander, Coughlin, Milkowski, & Boffetta, 2012; Habermeyer et al., 2015). One of them is the advantage that nitrite can transform into nitric oxide which has a preventive and therapeutic effect on many diseases in vivo (Habermeyer et al., 2015; Bryan et al., 2007), and the another disadvantage is that nitrite may react with amines in acidic medium to form nitrosamines which has carcinogenic risks in vivo (Erkekoglü & Baydar, 2010). Questionless, the excessive intake of nitrite has a significant correlation with occurrence of methemoglobin (Bryan et al., 2007), so a regulation from the World Health Organization (WHO) is that the nitrite content in drinking water should be controlled below 3 mg/L (WHO, 2017). Besides, the excessive nitrite content in natural water will cause serious impact on biology (Bulushi, Poole, Deeth, & Dykes, 2009).
At present, in the latest reports on determining trace amount of nitrite, there are electrochemical, photometric, fluorescent and chemluminescent methods, etc.

Mani, Prakash, and Chen (2012) reported an amperometric nitrite sensor which was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by reduced graphene oxide (rGO) which was reduced from graphene oxide (GO) by using hydrazine, Ag/AgCl was used as a reference electrode, and platinum wire was used as a counter electrode in their method. Employing rGO not only increased the electro-active surface but also the catalytic oxidation current of GCE. By using the catalytic activity of the rGO/GCE and the oxidation potential change from nitrite to nitrate, they determined nitrite in water samples in range 0.41–7.68 mg/L.

In order to enhance the electron-transfer ability of rGO/GCE, Radhakrishnan, Krishnamoorthy, Sekar, Wilson, and Kim (2014) modified the Fe2O3/rGO on GCE to form a Fe2O3/rGO modified electrode which was used to detect nitrite in tap water. So based on the catalysis of Fe2O3/rGO, the electrode in this method extended the detect range (0.0023–36 mg/L). Haldorai, Hwang et al. (2016), Haldorai, Kim et al. (2016) proposed another nitrite sensor, in which Fe2O3 was replaced by Co3O4, and then applying the cyclic voltammetry and the chrono-amperometry, the Co3O4/rGO electrode was used for detecting nitrite in water in the range of 0.046–17 mg/L.

Yang and Li (2014) utilized GO and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CATB) to synthesize the CTAB-GO complex under ultrasonic agitation, and formed CTAB-GO/multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) complex in chitosan/acetic acid solution. This CTAB-GO/MWCNT complex was modified on a GCE to fabricate a nitrite sensor which was used for the detection of nitrite in urine in range 0.23–37 mg/L.

Haldorai et al. immobilized Cytochrome-c (Cyt-c) on surface of MWCNT-TiN to form a modified electrode sensor for testing nitrite (Haldorai, Hwang et al., 2016; Haldorai, Kim et al., 2016). The role of TiN on the electrode was to increase the electric activity and to facilitate the immobilization effect of Cyt-c, which can further improve the electro-catalytic activity on the electrode, so the sensitivity of their method has been greatly improved (0.046–92 mg/L).

The single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) was used for nitrite detection. For example, the synthetic myoglobin/SWCNT complex and nafion solution were spread on the surface of graphite electrode to form the G/My-SWCNT/Nafion electrode (Turdean & Szabo, 2015). The nitrite in the range 23–230 mg/L can be quantified according to the current changes produced by translating nitrite into nitrate under the catalysis of iron ions in myoglobin on the electrode.

There are also many reports on the use of nanoparticles for indirectly determining nitrite. Ning, Zhang, and Zheng (2014) used sodium borohydride to reduce silver nitrate into silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) which formed the dendrimer with PAMAM in methanol solution. And then the dendrimer was modified on GCE to form a nitrite sensor which has been used to detect nitrite content in the range 0.18–66 mg/L in tap water and milk. The purpose of using AgNPs mainly was to improve the electrocatalytic activity and the surface area of this electrode.

Li et al. (2014) mixed graphite powder, TiO2 and 1-butylpyridine six fluorophosphates, and then which was added into Teflon holder of electrode to made a nano-TiO2/ionic liquid electrode which has a good electro-catalytic activity. TiO2 on the sensor surface improved its antifouling performance and increased its conductivity and surface area, thus the electro-catalytic activity of the sensor was improved. At last they detected nitrite in the sausage in the range 0.023–690 mg/L.

Some chromogenic methods have also been reported for detection of nitrites. Apyari and others developed a method for testing nitrite in combination with diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. In this method, polyurethane foam (PUF) was used as diazotization reagent, 3-hydroxy-7,8-benzo-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroquinoline (HBTHQ) was used as azo reagent, and a digital camera and a computer were used as a data processing device (Apyari, Dmitrienko, Ostrovskaya, Anaev, & Zolotov, 2008; Apyari, Dmitrienko, & Zolotov, 2011).

Using the diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, Luiz, Pezza, and Pezza (2012) determined nitrite in the range 0.3–5.0 mg/L by detecting the colored product on the filter-paper where the diazotization reaction was taken place among dapsone 4,4′-diamino-diphenyl sulphone and ethylenediamine hydrochloride with nitrite in acid medium. Khana Vilab and Tubino (2012) detected nitrite in cheese and meat based on a reaction between nitrite, sulfadiazine and α-naphthol to produce a coloured product, the light emitting diode (LED) was used as the light source and photosensitive resistors was used as the detector. Making use of nitrite to catalyze the reaction between potassium bromate and methyl red which was modified by 1-butyl-3-methimidazole six fluorophosphates ionic liquid, Zhang et al. (2014) quantified nitrite in food and river water in the range 4.0–192 μg/L according to its fading degree. Noor, Tan, Lee, Kwok, and Tajuddin (2016) immobilized safranine-O on butyl acrylate microspheres, and utilized an optical fiber reflectometer to detect nitrite in the range 10–100 μg/L based on the diazotization reaction between safranine-O and nitrite to produce the blue compounds in acidic media.

The fluorescence methods for determine nitrite were also reported. Guo, Zhang, Shangguang, and Zhen (2013) used nitrite to diazotize with an amine group on the o-phenylenediamin under acid conditions, a stronger fluorescent benzotriazole was generated after the covalent bond formed between diazotization group and the adjacent amino group, the hydroxypropyl-β cyclodextrin was complexed with benzotriazole to increase the sensitivity of testing nitrite. The linear range of the method was in 40–800 μg/L and the detection limit was 13.6 μg/L. Liu, Yang, Abdel-Halim, and Zhu (2013) employed nitrite to quench the fluorescence of gold nanoclusters to indirectly detect nitrite in the range 0.92–2300 μg/L. Based on a reaction which nitrite can enhance the fluorescence of pyridine derivative formed by 2-hydro-pyridine derivative in acidic medium, Wang et al. (2016) have also determined nitrite in the range 9.2–207 μg/L.

Carbon nanodots (CDs), which is a new kind of nanomaterial, has been widely used for its excellent luminescence property, low toxicity, biocompatibility, low cost and easy preparation (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015). Lin, Xue, Chen, and Lin (2011) synthesized CDs by using glycerol, polyethylene glycol and serine through microwave heating and constructed a NO2 −/H2O2/CDs chemiluminescence system which was applied to detect nitrite (4.6–460 μg/L) in pond water, river water and milk. Subsequently, based on this system, Lin, Dou, Li, Ma, and Lin (2015) further improved the sensitivity to detect nitrite by adding Na2CO3.

Besides, carbon nanodots formed by ethylenediamine (EDA) was also focused (Zhai et al., 2012) due to high fluorescence yield and amino acid content. It was found that under acidic condition, nitrite had observably quenching effect on fluorescence of EDA/CA-CDs, so in this research, a novel method for trace-level nitrite test was developed by us on the basis of the reaction system and the fluorescence capillary analysis (FCA) (Gao, Li, & Jiang, 2006; Li & Gao, 2007; Li, Liu, Gao, Chen, & Li, 2008; Zhao et al., 2008; Li, Du, Chen, & Gao, 2010; Li, Li, & Gao, 2015; Li, Li, Yang, & Gao, 2017), which is low cost, having green chemistry property, and suitable for the nitrite determination in micro-volume biological samples.

The main reagents used in this research were sodium nitrite, ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) and citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O, Chengdu, Kelong Chemical), and the related material was glass capillary (45 mm × 0.7 mm, West China glassware factory, Sichuan University). Water used in the experiment was ultrapure water (0.065 μS/cm), and the reagents used were all analytical purity.

The main instruments used in this experiment were a RF-5031PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), an AB204-N electronic analytical balance (Mettler, Toledo), a WP800T microwave (Galanz, Chengdu), a TP3001 thermometer (Waytop, Nanjing), a CM-230 water meter (Aike, Chengdu), a HI1290 pH (Hanna, Italy) and a K30 thermostat (Orsus, Hangzhou).

0.150 g sodium nitrite was weighed and dissolved by water, and then was transferred into a 100-mL volumetric flask to dilute to the tick mark, and stored in 4 °C. When used, it was diluted to the required concentration.

2.10 g of C6H8O7·H2O was dissolved in 20 mL pure water in a 100-mL beaker, and 2.0 g of ethylenediamine was dropwise added in stirring. The beaker was put into one 800 W microwave oven after full blending to heat at the medium scale of the microwave oven, and stopped after about 4 min (the solution in the beaker become brown and yellow sticky matter). And then the beaker was put in a vacuum dryer at 60 °C for one hour to remove the residual small molecules therein. Finally, 500 mL deionized water was added into the beaker to dissolve the sticky matter (Hu et al., 2014; Qu, Wang, Lu, Liu, & Wang, 2012). 1.5 mL of EDA/CA-CDs solution was transferred in 500-mL volumetric flask to volume with hydrochloric acid (pH1.5).

The principle for determining nitrite was shown in Fig. 1 . Nitrite can transform into nitrosonium cation ([ON]+) in acidic conditions (Erkekoglü & Baydar, 2010; Jin et al., 2017). There are lots of primary amine groups (NH2) on the surface of CA/EDA-CDs, and the amine groups has lone pair electrons, the nitrosonium cation has very strong attracting force for electron, so the nitrosonium cation can combined with NH2 on the surface of CA/EDA-CDs to produce N-nitroamine. The amine carries lone pair electrons and [ON]+ has the strong attraction of electrons. At the same time, this process accompanied the electron-transfer, in which the electrons on the surface of the CA/EDA-CDs do not return to the ground state from the excited state, this will cause the quenching of CA/EDA-CDs fluorescence. Different nitrite concentration will cause different quenching for fluorescence of CA/EDA-CDs. Therefore, the quenching extent can be used to quantify the concentration of nitrite in samples.

In a 1-mL EP tube, 200 μL acidic CA/EDA-CDs mixed with 200 μL nitrite standard solution (or real samples), and then reacted for 20 min. Using the capillarity, the glass capillary inhaled the mixture (18 μL), then it was inserted into a home-made capillary holder which was installed in the dark chamber of the fluorophotometer, and its fluorescence intensity (F) was detected at wavelengths of 370 nm excitation and 460 nm emission. Using ultrapure water to replace the standard (or sample solution) above-mentioned and again mixed with the same volume of the CA/EDA-CDs reagent, the fluorescence intensity (F 0) of the mixture was measured in the same way and the F 0 value was used as the blank signal, and the fluorescence quenching extent (ΔF = F 0 − F) was proportional to the concentration of nitrite in the test sample.

5.0 g the test sample containing nitrite was taken to grind into homogenate solution, which was transferred in a 250-mL cone bottle where 12.5 mL of the saturated borax solution (50 g/L) and about 150 mL of water around 70 °C were added, and then the cone bottle was heated for 15 min and put into a cold water bath to cool to room temperature. The extracted liquid in the cone bottle was transferred into a 200-mL volumetric flask, where 5 mL of 106 g/L potassium ferrocyanide and 5 mL of 220 g/L zinc acetate were added in order and diluted to the marker with water. Addition purpose of zinc acetate was to precipitate protein in the extracted liquid. The supernatant in the volumetric flask was placed for 30 min and filtered by filter paper. And then discarding initial 30 mL of filtrate and adding 5 g of granular active carbon to mix for 5 min, the filtrate was again filtered, and the final filtrate obtained was preserved in 4 °C (GB/T 5009, 2016; He, Zhang, Huang, & Hu, 2007; Chetty & Prasad, 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectrum related to the reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: Under conditions that band-pass widths of the excitation and emission light were 5.0 nm and 20 nm, respectively, at first, the fluorescence spectrum of CA/EDA-CDs was scanned, and the obtained results were shown as Fig. 2 a. It can be seen that CA/EDA-CDs has the maximum excitation wavelength at 370 nm and the maximum emission wavelength at 460 nm. Under acidic conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CA/EDA-CDs was reduced to 1/3, and when the nitrite was added into CA/EDA-CDs, the fluorescence was significantly quenched, this also reconfirmed the reaction mechanism in Fig. 1. Subsequently, different concentrations of nitrite were mixed with CA/EDA-CDs (pH2.0) by the same volume, and the spectral scanning was also conducted to the mixture which was placed at room temperature after 30 min. From the result shown in Fig. 2b, it can be seen that with the increase of the nitrite concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CA/EDA-CDs was decreased, namely, its quenched extent was enhanced.

To confirm the reaction mechanism, first, according to the synthesis steps of “ethylenediamine/citric acid” nanodots, solutions of “ethylenediamine/citric acid” and “citric acid” were respectively heated, then dissolved and diluted with water by the same multiplier. Finally, their fluorescence spectrums were scanned under the same optical band. The results as Fig. 2c–d showed that, under the same conditions, the fluorescence intensity of nanodots with nitrogen was larger than that of nitrogen nanodots without nitrogen, which indicated that nitrogen played an important role in forming nanodots. The results also verified our proposed mechanism.

The reaction involved in the method must be in an acidic condition, but when the reaction solution is in the higher acidity (Baruah, Deka, & Chowdhury, 2014), it severely results in the quenching of CA/EDA-CDs fluorescence itself, which will not only lead to the waste of CA/EDA-CDs reagent, but also affects the detection sensitivity of nitrite. However, the lower acidity of the reaction solution will also lead to reduce the reaction rate of the CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − system, and this will affect the test throughput of the method. Therefore, the effect of CA/EDA-CDs’s pH was investigated in this experiment. The test conditions were that CA/EDA-CDs concentration was 12 mg/L (1/300 stoste), the excitation and emission wavelengths were 370 nm and 460 nm, the CA/EDA-CDs reagent and sodium nitrite solution was mixed by 1:1, the reaction time was 30 min and the reaction temperature was 25 °C. The results obtained were shown in Fig. 3 a. From the change trend of the two curves, it can be known that when acidity of CA/EDA-CDs solution was about pH1.5, the fluorescence quenching extent was the largest, namely, the sensitivity for testing nitrite was the highest under this acidity. Therefore, the acidity of CA/EDA-CDs was chose at pH 1.5 in the method.

The CA/EDA-CDs concentration was calculated according to the concentration of ethylenediamine, and the effect of CA/EDA-CDs in different concentrations on determining nitrite was investigated. In the experiment, different concentrations of the CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − reaction solution were diluted by the HCl solution (pH 1.5).

Because the CA/EDA-CDs concentration is different, its blank fluorescence intensity is different, so the band-pass widths to be used should be also different. Therefore, the band-pass widths corresponding to 180, 72, 36, 18, 12, 9, 7.2 and 6 mg/L of CA/EDA-CDs solutions were respectively selected at 1.5/20 nm, 3.0/10 nm, 5.0/10 nm, 5.0/15 nm, 5.0/20 nm and 10.0/20 nm of excitation/emission wavelengths to make the experiments, obtained results were shown in Fig. 3b.

It can be seen that there was a little difference between 0.012 g/L and 0.009 g/L CA/EDA-CDs in the reacting solution, but the band-pass widths corresponding to 0.012 g/L of CA/EDA-CDs solution was smaller than that corresponding to 0.009 g/L CA/EDA-CDs solution. So 0.012 g/L of the CA/EDA-CDs solution was selected to be as the reaction solution in the method.

Under the selected CA/EDA-CDs concentration, dosage of CA/EDA-CDs for assaying nitrite was examined, and the results obtained were shown in Fig. 3c. The change trend of two curves displayed that when the sample volume was 1 mL and the CA/EDA-CDs reagent mixed with the sample according to the volume of 1:1, the fluorescence quenching signal was the largest. Namely, the detection sensitivity was the highest under the condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature for reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: In the method based on fluorescence quenching, if its reaction temperature was too low, nitrite would be unable to completely quench fluorescence of CA/EDA-CDs reagent, and if the temperature was too high, the self-quenching of the CA/EDA-CDs fluorescence would occur. Therefore, under above-selected conditions, the CA/EDA-CDs reagent and the NO2 − samples were mixed by 1:1 proportional, effect of the reaction temperature on quenched degree of CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − fluorescence was also investigated. From results shown in Fig. 3d, it can see that when the reaction temperature was at 60 °C, the fluorescence quenching related to the nitrite concentration was the maximum. But, for the convenience of testing, room temperatures about 20–30 °C were selected as the reaction temperature under the premise with sufficient sensitivity.

The nitrite quenching the fluorescence of CA/EDA-CDs belongs to the slow reaction in this method. This is because nitrite must be first reacted with acidic reagent to form the onium ion, where there is a chemical reaction equilibrium which is affected by temperature. Therefore, the reaction time was also investigated under the conditions above-mentioned. From the curve trend of 0.5 mg/L nitrite in Fig. 3e, it was known that when the reaction had undergone for 20 min, the fluorescence of the CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − reaction system was no longer changed, namely, the reaction had been completed. But the time of high nitrite concentration (2 mg/L) reached the reaction equilibrium was shorter. By synthetically considering, the reaction time was chosen 20 min in the subsequent experiments.

Because the method was based on the fluorescence quenching, firstly the fluorescence blank value of the CA/EDA-CDs reagent must be obtained. So, the fluorescence blank of CA/EDA-CDs changing with time was investigated. The curve trend in Fig. 3f showed that the blank value tended to be stable after 0.5 min, therefore, the time for detecting the blank in the method was selected to be greater than 0.5 min.

Under the selected CA/EDA-CDs concentration, effect of the acidity in nitrite solution was investigated, and results shown as Fig. 4 a were obtained. The red line with triangles was the result using NaOH and HCl solutions to adjust acidity of the nitrite solution, and the black line with circles was the result of using the HCl solution to adjust the nitrite solution which contained borax. We can see that the fluorescence quenching extents of the CA/EDA-CDs reagent with or without borax were all the same at pH 6. This means that the real samples before determining has to adjust its acidity to pH 6. In addition, when the nitrite standards with borax were mixed with acidic CA/EDA-CDs reagent, due to the acidity of the reaction system was slightly changed, so nitrite quenching the extent of CA/EDA-CDs fluorescence was the same approximately. The reason from the bending of the black curves with circles was that nitrite samples containing borax was alkaline, which lowered the acidity of the CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − system, so resulted in change in fluorescence quenching conditions, these made the determination result to be lowered.

Due to there are some substances with fluorescence and color in the some real samples, which could interfere with the nitrite determination, so granular and powdered active carbon was used to investigate the eliminating effect for the interference. Different amount of powdered or granular activated carbon was added to nitrite samples containing 3 g/L borax, and then these samples were filtered after placing for 5 min. The filtrates were mixed with the CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − reagent by 1:1 ratio to detect their fluorescence quenching values according to the above operation method, and the obtained results were shown in Fig. 4b–d. Fig. 4b indicated that the powdered active carbon itself affected the nitrite determination and the effect was greater in determining nitrite of the lower concentration. But it can be known from Fig. 4c, if the addition of granular active carbon in the samples, itself was not interfered with the nitrite determination.

Some ions were added in the CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − (0.5 mg/L) reaction system to evaluate their interference. The obtained results were shown in Table 1 . The therein data showed that the relative deviation ((ΔF2-ΔF1)/ΔF1) formed by coexisting ions was less than 4.2% under the preconditions of various ion concentration being in the added ranges and carbonate concentration being not more than 100 mg/L. In order to eliminate the interference of carbonate ion in high concentration to the nitrite determination, the samples containing nitrite must adjust to pH7.0 before testing.

Under optimal conditions, the linear correlation between the fluorescence quenching degree of CA/EDA-CDs (ΔF) and the nitrite concentration was investigated in range 20–500 μg/L. Experimental results manifested that the linear relationship between both was better (ΔF = 965.0c NO2 − + 0.3, r = 0.9985). And then the repeatability for this method was estimated by eleven repeatedly detecting 50 μg/L NO2 −, in which the obtained result was ΔF = 46.3 ± 2.1 (45.0, 45.8, 43.8, 45.9, 46.3, 48.3, 51.5, 46.3, 46.0, 45.3, 44.8) and the calculated RSD value was less than 4.5%. The detection limit was 6.5 μg/L (3SD/k = 3 × 2.1/965).

In order to confirm the linear range and detection limit for the CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − reaction system, the fluorescence cell with 10 mm optical path was also used to detect the nitrite in the same conditions which were that the CA/EDA-CDs concentration was 0.45 mg/L and the band-passes of the excitation and emission were fixed at 10 nm and 20 nm, respectively. The results showed that the linear relationship was good in the range 2.0–40 μg/L (ΔF = 9.48c NO2 − + 13.8, r = 0.998). Then repeatability of the fluorescence-cell method was evaluated by using 2 μg/L as a testing sample, in which the detected mean ΔF was 40.3 ± 2.0 (37.2, 38.3, 40.6, 41.0, 39.3, 37.8, 39.9, 41.0, 42.0, 42.9 and 42.9) and RSD was less than 4.86%; the detection limit was 0.63 μg/L (3SD/k = 3 × 2.0/9.48).

In order to verify the applicability of the Stern-Volmer equation, the data from this study were substituted into the equation to form a related equation between fluorescence quenching and nitrite concentration, which was F0/F = 3.09c NO2 − + 0.81 (r = 0.987). Comparing with our relationship equation of ΔF = 965.0c NO2 − + 0.3 (r = 0.995), it can be seen that the linear correlation of Stern-Volmer equation is not good and the intercept is also large, so the Stern-Volmer equation was not used for determination of the nitrite content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tests on real samples and comparison
ce:section.ce:para: Three different brands of sausages were chosen in nearby supermarkets as test samples. The sample No.1 is Meihao sausage (Xingxiwang Co, Chengdu), the sample No.2 is Jinluo sausage (Linyi Wenrui Food Co., Shandong), and the sample No.3 is Shuanghui sausage (Shuanghui food Co., Ltd., Mianyang). The acidity of the extracted sample solutions were adjusted to pH 7.0 by HCl or NaOH solutions, and the FCA and fluorescent-cell methods were used respectively to detect these samples, the obtained data were shown in Table 2 . To evaluate accuracy of the tested results, the spectrophotometry with N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine reagent (GB/T 5009, 2016) was used for comparing experiments, which had the linear equation: A = 0.901c NO2 − (mg/L) + 0.008. The related data in Table 2 showed that there was no significant difference between results from two methods, this also proved that the FCA method was reliable.

In order to further evaluate the anti-interference ability of the FCA method, a recovery experiment was carried out. According to the obtained results in Table 3 , the addition recovery for the FCA method was in the range of 95–105% which was satisfactory and can be used for the accurate determination of trace nitrite in food and water.

Based on new fluorescence quenching of the CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − reaction system and the FCA, a new method for determining nitrite was established. The FCA method only needed an eco-friendly, high-sensitive, easy-synthesis fluorescence reagent of CA/EDA-CDs to can achieve the purpose for the accurate determination of trace-level nitrite in foods and nature water. The glass capillary in the approach can be used as a micro-volume container for the reaction of CA/EDA-CDs/NO2 − system and for the detection in the FCA method. Typical advantages of our method are the simple operation, little sample and reagent consumptions and significant reduction in little chemical waste.
